name: architecture
context: MCP architecture deep dive ‚Äî hosts, clients, servers, data layer, transport layer, lifecycle, and capability negotiation
info: MCP follows a layered client-server architecture. The host (an AI application like Claude Desktop or VS Code) creates MCP clients ‚Äî one per server connection. Each client maintains a dedicated connection to an MCP server over a transport (stdio or Streamable HTTP). The architecture has two layers: the data layer (JSON-RPC 2.0 protocol with lifecycle management and server/client primitives) and the transport layer (message framing, connection management, authentication). The data layer defines three server primitives (tools, resources, prompts) and two client primitives (sampling and elicitation). The protocol is stateful, beginning with an initialization handshake where both sides negotiate capabilities, followed by an operation phase of request/response/notification exchange, and ending with shutdown.
body:
# MCP Architecture

The Model Context Protocol follows a layered client-server architecture designed for AI applications to connect to external systems through a standardized protocol.

## Participants

### Host

The AI application that coordinates MCP. Examples:
- Claude Desktop
- Claude Code
- VS Code (Copilot)
- Cursor
- ChatGPT
- Custom chatbots

The host creates and manages MCP clients ‚Äî one per server connection. It decides which servers to connect to, how to present tools/resources/prompts to users, and how to route LLM tool calls.

### Client

A component within the host that maintains a dedicated connection to one MCP server. The client handles:
- Transport connection (stdio subprocess or HTTP)
- Initialization handshake and capability negotiation
- Sending requests (tools/list, tools/call, resources/read, prompts/get)
- Receiving responses and notifications
- Exposing client primitives (sampling, elicitation) to the server

### Server

A program that provides capabilities to clients. Servers can be:
- **Local** ‚Äî Launched as subprocesses via stdio transport (e.g., filesystem server)
- **Remote** ‚Äî Running as HTTP services via Streamable HTTP transport (e.g., Sentry MCP)

Servers expose three primitives: [[mcp/server/tools]], [[mcp/server/resources]], [[mcp/server/prompts]].

```
‚îå‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îê
‚îÇ           Host (AI App)             ‚îÇ
‚îÇ                                     ‚îÇ
‚îÇ  ‚îå‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îê  ‚îå‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îê        ‚îÇ
‚îÇ  ‚îÇ Client 1 ‚îÇ  ‚îÇ Client 2 ‚îÇ  ...   ‚îÇ
‚îÇ  ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚î¨‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îò  ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚î¨‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îò        ‚îÇ
‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îº‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îº‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îò
        ‚îÇ              ‚îÇ
   ‚îå‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚ñº‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îê    ‚îå‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚ñº‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îê
   ‚îÇServer A ‚îÇ    ‚îÇServer B ‚îÇ
   ‚îÇ (stdio) ‚îÇ    ‚îÇ (HTTP)  ‚îÇ
   ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îò    ‚îî‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚îò
```

## Layers

### Data Layer (Inner)

Implements JSON-RPC 2.0 protocol defining message structure and semantics:

**Lifecycle Management:**
- `initialize` ‚Äî Client sends protocol version and capabilities; server responds with its capabilities
- `notifications/initialized` ‚Äî Client signals readiness
- Operation phase ‚Äî Normal request/response/notification exchange
- Shutdown ‚Äî Connection terminated (stdin closed or HTTP DELETE)

**Server Primitives:**
- **[[mcp/server/tools]]** ‚Äî Model-controlled functions (`tools/list`, `tools/call`)
- **[[mcp/server/resources]]** ‚Äî Application-controlled data (`resources/list`, `resources/read`, `resources/subscribe`)
- **[[mcp/server/prompts]]** ‚Äî User-controlled templates (`prompts/list`, `prompts/get`)

**Client Primitives:**
- **Sampling** ‚Äî Server requests LLM completions from the client (`sampling/complete`)
- **Elicitation** ‚Äî Server requests user input from the client (`elicitation/request`)
- **Logging** ‚Äî Server sends log messages to the client

**Utility Features:**
- Notifications ‚Äî Real-time updates (tools/list_changed, resources/updated)
- Progress tracking ‚Äî Long-running operation updates
- Pagination ‚Äî Cursor-based pagination for list operations
- Completion ‚Äî Auto-complete for prompt arguments and resource templates

### Transport Layer (Outer)

Manages communication channels between clients and servers:

- **[[mcp/transport]]** ‚Äî stdio (local subprocess) and Streamable HTTP (remote HTTP+SSE)
- **[[mcp/auth]]** ‚Äî OAuth 2.1 authorization for HTTP transport
- Connection establishment, message framing, session management

## Lifecycle

```
1. INITIALIZE
   Client ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚Üí Server: initialize(protocolVersion, capabilities, clientInfo)
   Server ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚Üí Client: InitializeResult(protocolVersion, capabilities, serverInfo)
   Client ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚Üí Server: notifications/initialized

2. OPERATE
   Client ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚Üí Server: tools/list, tools/call, resources/read, prompts/get
   Server ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚Üí Client: responses, notifications
   Server ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚Üí Client: sampling/complete, elicitation/request (client primitives)

3. SHUTDOWN
   Client closes stdin (stdio) or sends DELETE (HTTP)
```

## Capability Negotiation

During initialization, both sides declare what they support:

**Server capabilities:**
```json
{
  "capabilities": {
    "tools": { "listChanged": true },
    "resources": { "subscribe": true, "listChanged": true },
    "prompts": { "listChanged": true }
  }
}
```

**Client capabilities:**
```json
{
  "capabilities": {
    "sampling": {},
    "elicitation": {},
    "roots": { "listChanged": true }
  }
}
```

Capabilities determine which methods are available. If a server doesn't declare `tools`, the client must not send `tools/list` or `tools/call`.

## JSON-RPC 2.0 Message Types

### Request
```json
{"jsonrpc": "2.0", "id": 1, "method": "tools/list", "params": {}}
```

### Response
```json
{"jsonrpc": "2.0", "id": 1, "result": {"tools": [...]}}
```

### Error
```json
{"jsonrpc": "2.0", "id": 1, "error": {"code": -32602, "message": "Unknown tool"}}
```

### Notification (no id, no response expected)
```json
{"jsonrpc": "2.0", "method": "notifications/tools/list_changed"}
```

## Protocol Versioning

MCP protocol versions use date-based identifiers (e.g., `2025-06-18`). During initialization, client and server negotiate a mutually compatible version. The client includes `MCP-Protocol-Version` header on HTTP requests.

## Control Flow Patterns

### Tool Use Flow
```
User ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚Üí Host ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚Üí LLM: "What's the weather?"
LLM ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚Üí Host: tool_use(get_weather, {city: "SF"})
Host ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚Üí Client ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚Üí Server: tools/call(get_weather, {city: "SF"})
Server ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚Üí Client ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚Üí Host: result({content: [{type: "text", text: "72¬∞F"}]})
Host ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚Üí LLM: tool_result
LLM ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚Üí Host ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚Üí User: "It's 72¬∞F in San Francisco."
```

### Resource Enrichment Flow
```
User selects resource in UI
Host ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚Üí Client ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚Üí Server: resources/read("schema://db")
Server ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚Üí Client ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚Üí Host: contents({text: "CREATE TABLE..."})
Host adds resource content to LLM context
```

### Prompt Invocation Flow
```
User types /code_review
Host ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚Üí Client ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚Üí Server: prompts/get("code_review", {code: "..."})
Server ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚Üí Client ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚Üí Host: messages([{role: "user", content: "Review this..."}])
Host sends prompt messages to LLM
```

## Design Principles

1. **Separation of concerns** ‚Äî Protocol (data layer) is independent of transport
2. **Capability negotiation** ‚Äî Both sides declare what they support
3. **Stateful connections** ‚Äî Lifecycle management ensures ordered initialization
4. **Dynamic discovery** ‚Äî Capabilities can change at runtime via notifications
5. **Transport agnostic** ‚Äî Same JSON-RPC messages work over stdio and HTTP
6. **Security by design** ‚Äî Human-in-the-loop for tool calls, audience-bound tokens

sections.title: Host, Client, and Server roles
sections.body: The **Host** is the AI application (Claude Desktop, VS Code) that manages MCP clients. The **Client** is a protocol component within the host that maintains a dedicated connection to one server ‚Äî handling initialization, capability negotiation, and message routing. The **Server** is an external program that exposes tools, resources, and prompts. One host can connect to many servers simultaneously, each via its own client instance. Local servers use stdio transport; remote servers use Streamable HTTP.

sections.title: Data layer (JSON-RPC protocol)
sections.body: The inner layer implementing JSON-RPC 2.0 with four concerns: **Lifecycle management** (initialization handshake, capability negotiation, shutdown). **Server primitives** (tools for actions, resources for data, prompts for templates). **Client primitives** (sampling for LLM access, elicitation for user input, logging for debug messages). **Utilities** (notifications for real-time updates, progress tracking, pagination, auto-completion). All messages follow JSON-RPC format with `jsonrpc`, `id`, `method`, `params`, `result`, or `error` fields.

sections.title: Capability negotiation
sections.body: During initialization, client and server exchange capability declarations. The server declares which primitives it supports (tools, resources, prompts) and optional features (listChanged, subscribe). The client declares which client primitives it supports (sampling, elicitation, roots). Each capability is an object ‚Äî its presence means support, and nested fields configure optional features. Capabilities constrain which methods can be called ‚Äî e.g., if a server doesn't declare `tools`, the client must not send `tools/list`.

sections.title: JSON-RPC message types
sections.body: Four message types: **Requests** have `id`, `method`, and optional `params` ‚Äî they expect a response. **Responses** match a request by `id` and contain `result` or `error`. **Errors** include a numeric `code` (-32602 for invalid params, -32603 for internal error) and a `message` string. **Notifications** have `method` and optional `params` but no `id` ‚Äî they don't expect a response. All messages are JSON-RPC 2.0 with `jsonrpc: "2.0"` field.

sections.title: Control flow patterns
sections.body: Three primary flows: **Tool use** ‚Äî User query ‚Üí LLM selects tool ‚Üí Host routes to Client ‚Üí Client calls Server ‚Üí Result flows back ‚Üí LLM generates response. **Resource enrichment** ‚Äî User or application selects a resource ‚Üí Client reads from Server ‚Üí Content added to LLM context. **Prompt invocation** ‚Äî User selects a prompt (slash command) ‚Üí Client gets prompt from Server ‚Üí Messages sent to LLM. Each flow demonstrates the separation between Host (orchestration), Client (protocol), and Server (execution).

links.context: Servers expose primitives through the data layer
Clients connect to servers and route protocol messages
Transport layer handles connection and message framing

author: dojo-community
